edge: Large scenes with many rigid objects are crucial for applications like robotics, engineering, film, and video games.
Rigid interactions are difficult to model due to small changes in initial state or simulation parameters leading to large changes in the final state.
Recently developed learned simulators based on GNNs provide an alternative to hand-designed simulators like MuJoCo and PyBullet.
Learned simulators accurately capture dynamics of real objects directly from real-world observations.
Current learned simulators operate on meshes and scale poorly to scenes with many objects or detailed shapes.
SDF-Sim is the first learned rigid-body simulator designed for scale, leveraging SDFs to represent object shapes and improve distance computation speed.
The simulator is designed to avoid the bottleneck of collision detection faced by previous simulators.
GNN-based simulators can be scaled to scenes with hundreds of objects and up to 1.1 million nodes.
SDF-Sim can be applied to real world scenes by extracting SDFs from multi-view images.
Traditional physics simulators face a significant challenge when dealing with tables, plates, and glasses due to the need for precise 3D shape, location, and physical parameters of each object.

Learned simulators based on graph networks are able to learn physical properties directly from real data, leading to a powerful alternative to traditional hand-designed simulators like MuJoCo and PyBullet.
Graph networks have the capability to capture a range of complex physical dynamics and generalize to new scenes.
Current GNN-based methods do not scale well to large scenes, similar to traditional simulators, due to the reliance on 3D meshes to describe object shapes.
In scenes with a large number of objects or detailed meshes, collision detection between objects becomes extremely costly, impacting runtime and memory performance.
Large meshes in input graphs can result in hundreds of thousands of nodes and edges, affecting runtime and memory performance in terms of graph networks.
To address the issue with expensive collision detection, a common approach in classic simulation literature is to use signed-distance functions or fields (SDFs) to represent objects implicitly.
SDFs implicitly represent the object shape and the distance field.
SDFs parameterized by MLPs are learned for each object to implicitly represent the object shape and the distance field.
A GNN-based simulator uses learned SDFs to predict object dynamics for the next simulation step.
distance queries are a main driver of the compute cost in traditional rigid body simulation.

learned SDFs store the shape implicitly in the MLP weights and are fast to train and query.
learned SDFs are less memory-hungry compared to 3D grids, making them a perfect candidate for simulation.

node_2: computer graphics, game engines, and robotics
edge: Signed distance functions (SDFs) are widely used in computer graphics, game engines, and robotics for fast collision detection and computation of distances to an object.
SDFs define a field representing the signed distance from an arbitrary point to the closest point on the surface of the object.
SDFs permit constant-time queries of the distance to an object surface, irrespective of the number of nodes/faces in the object mesh.
Object surface is an essential component of collision detection.
Graph edges are constructed in SDF-Sim using the object surface.
SDF is often pre-computed as a high-resolution 3D grid containing signed distances from the points on the grid to the object surface.
Grid SDF allows to speed up distance queries by trading off memory.
Grid SDF contains signed distances from the points on the grid to the object surface.
Learned SDFs gain popularity for the reconstruction of water-tight 3D shapes from images.
MLP parameterized by θ approximates the continuous distance field f(y; θ).
Learned SDFs have not been explored enough to speed up physical simulations.
Limited available works combine learned SDFs with classic physics solvers.
Learned SDFs take ∼32x less memory than traditional 3D-voxel-grid SDFs.
SDF allows us to easily compute the closest point in 3D space on the object surface.

The closest point y∗ on the object surface is represented by the equation y∗ = y − fθ(y)∇fθ(y), where fθ(y) is the distance between y and y∗ and ∇fθ(y) points in the opposite direction of the shortest path from y to the surface of the object.
The distance fθ(y) is the distance between y and the closest point y∗ on the object surface.
The gradient ∇fθ(y) points in the opposite direction of the shortest path from y to the surface of the object, and is unit-norm.
This calculation of the closest points for collision resolution in the simulation requires only one forward and one backward pass of the network.
SDF-Sim is a graph-network-based simulator that uses learned SDFs to represent object shapes and perform fast and memory-efficient distance computation between the objects.
SDF fθ(y) is an MLP that takes in a 3D point y ∈ R3 and outputs a scalar SDF value for the object.
Training SDF functions per object involves representing SDF fθ(y) as an MLP that takes in a 3D point y ∈ R3 and outputs a scalar SDF value.

By leveraging SDF properties, a new way is proposed to construct the input graph for the graph network, allowing for the use of a smaller graph size and achieving an order-of-magnitude reduction in runtime and memory on large simulations.

Surface nodes {nik} carry information about object motion, such as impulses from collision events, and this information is propagated to the object node oi. Additionally, the surface edges between the nodes {nik} can be omitted without loss of accuracy as shown by [19].

In graph networks, nodes and edges are associated with feature vectors that hold information about the motion and relation between the nodes. These feature vectors are necessary for simulation purposes.
Node features for surface nodes nik are computed as finite-difference velocity estimates in the simulation. These features are essential for constructing the necessary node features for simulation.

SDF-Sim requires significantly fewer edges than mesh-based methods due to a single collision edge testing a node against an entire object surface.
SDF-Sim has O(I · K) edges in the worst case, a much smaller number than O(K^2) for mesh-based simulators, allowing for scalability to large scenes.

node_1: simulations with a variable number of spheres
edge: A set of simulations with a variable number of spheres, ranging from 1 to 512, are created to study the scaling properties of the model.
The ground-truth for these scenes is created using PyBullet simulator.
SDF-Sim has an order-of-magnitude fewer collision edges compared to FIGNet* and FIGNet.
FIGNet and FIGNet* run out of memory for simulations with >140 and >160 objects respectively.
SDF-Sim can simulate the entire set of 512 interacting objects using the same Nvidia V100 GPU.
SDF-Sim is up to 5 times faster than FIGNet* in terms of total runtime.

Figure 8(b) illustrates the rollout errors at different steps of the simulation, with SDF-Sim showing better generalization to larger scenes and lower error rates compared to FIGNet*.

node_2: lower error on datasets with smaller scenes
edge: FIGNet* exhibits lower error rates on datasets featuring smaller scenes attributed to its training on Movi-B, which includes a sphere object missing in Movi-C dataset.
Despite not being trained on a sphere object, SDF-Sim performs exceptionally well on both penetration and rollout metrics, showcasing its ability to generalize effectively over different geometries.
